summary: Practices the imperfect -aba ending with new vocabulary, introduces reflexive/self verbs for intransitive meanings (se quemaba, se rompe), and teaches the demonstrative adjectives este/estos/ese/esos for masculine nouns.

teachings.summary: Practiced the imperfect -aba endings with new verbs cenar and quemar.

examples.english: I was having dinner / He was having dinner

teachings.summary: When a verb's action isn't directed at something else, Spanish uses 'se' (itself) to show the subject is affected by its own action. This avoids leaving the sentence feeling incomplete. 'La cena quemaba' feels incomplete (what was the dinner burning?), but 'La cena se quemaba' means the dinner was burning/getting burnt.
teachings.rule: Use se + verb when the subject undergoes its own action (it breaks = se rompe, it burns = se quema). Without se, listeners expect to hear what the action is directed at.

examples.english: I get burnt in the sun

examples.spanish: Esos se rompen todo el tiempo
examples.english: Those ones break all the time

teachings.notes: Word order is flexible: La cena se quemaba = Se quemaba la cena. Without se, the verb expects an object.

teachings.summary: Taught este (this) and estos (these) for masculine nouns, showing the logic: este contains the /e/ of el, estos contains the /os/ of los. That/those formed by removing the t: ese/esos.
teachings.rule: This: este (masc sing), estos (masc plural). That: ese (masc sing), esos (masc plural). Mnemonic: este has /e/ of el, estos has /os/ of los. Remove t for that/those.
teachings.notes: The pattern este/estos maps to el/los. Remove t to get ese/esos (that/those).
